subject: Economics
paper_name: Edexcel Economics A Paper 1: Markets and Business Behaviour (June 2022)

sections.name: Section A: Data Response — UK Supermarkets
Section B: Data Response — UK Labour Market

questions.question_text: With reference to Extract A, identify two significant features of the data shown.
questions.extract_text: Extract A shows market share data for UK supermarkets from 2019 to 2022. Tesco's market share fell from 27.4% to 26.9%, while Aldi's share rose from 7.9% to 9.3% and Lidl's from 5.9% to 7.1%. The total value of the UK grocery market grew from £203bn to £228bn over the period. Average food prices increased by 16.4% in the year to March 2023, the highest rate in 45 years.

questions.question_text: With reference to the information provided, explain how the characteristics of an oligopoly are demonstrated in the UK supermarket industry.
With reference to Extract B and your own knowledge, assess the extent to which the growth of discount supermarkets has increased contestability in the UK grocery market.
questions.extract_text: Extract B: Aldi and Lidl have opened over 400 new stores in the UK since 2015, investing £3.5bn. Their combined market share has more than doubled from 8% to 16.4% between 2015 and 2022. Aldi's average basket price is estimated to be 20% lower than the 'Big Four' supermarkets. However, barriers to entry remain significant — a new full-size supermarket requires investment of £30m-£50m, planning permission takes 12-18 months, and established retailers control the most desirable locations.

questions.question_text: With reference to the extracts and your own knowledge, evaluate the impact of non-price competition on consumer welfare in the UK supermarket industry.
Evaluate the view that the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) is effective in promoting competition and protecting consumers in oligopolistic markets such as the UK supermarket industry.
With reference to Extract C, identify two significant features of the data shown.
questions.extract_text: Extract C shows UK labour market data from 2019 to 2022. The unemployment rate fell from 3.8% in Q4 2019 to 3.5% in Q3 2022. Job vacancies reached a record 1.3 million in March 2022. Average weekly earnings growth was 6.0% in nominal terms but -2.5% in real terms in Q3 2022. The economic inactivity rate increased from 20.4% to 21.6% over the period, with over 500,000 additional people classified as long-term sick.

questions.question_text: With reference to the information provided, explain how the concepts of derived demand and wage determination are relevant to understanding the current UK labour market.
With reference to Extract D and your own knowledge, assess the impact of the National Living Wage on employment and income inequality in the UK.
questions.extract_text: Extract D: The National Living Wage increased from £8.91 to £9.50 in April 2022, with a further increase to £10.42 in April 2023. The Low Pay Commission estimated that the NLW directly increased wages for 2 million workers. Research by the London School of Economics found no significant negative impact on employment levels. However, the Federation of Small Businesses reported that 38% of small firms found the increase 'very difficult' to absorb, with some reducing hours rather than making redundancies.

questions.question_text: With reference to the extracts and your own knowledge, evaluate the effectiveness of education and training policies in addressing labour market failures in the UK.
Evaluate the view that trade unions improve economic outcomes for workers without causing significant labour market distortions.
Evaluate the view that third-degree price discrimination always benefits producers more than consumers. Refer to at least one diagram in your answer.
Evaluate the view that perfect competition leads to the most efficient allocation of resources compared to other market structures. Refer to at least one diagram in your answer.
